title: Freshwater use by aggregated region
subtitle: Global freshwater withdrawals for agricultural, industrial and domestic uses by aggregated regional groupings. OECD members are defined as countries who were members in 2010 and their membership was carried back in time. BRICS countries are Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa. ROW refers to the Rest of the World, excluding OECD and BRICS countries.
caption:
Freshwater use by aggregated region

Global freshwater withdrawals for agricultural, industrial and domestic uses by aggregated regional groupings. OECD members are defined as countries who were members in 2010 and their membership was carried back in time. BRICS countries are Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa. ROW refers to the Rest of the World, excluding OECD and BRICS countries.

This stacked area chart tracks global freshwater withdrawals from 1901 to 2010, measured in cubic meters. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 4 trillion cubic meters, while the horizontal axis marks the timeline in ten-year increments. The data is divided into three aggregated regions: OECD (blue, bottom layer), BRICS (red, middle layer), and ROW (green, top layer). In 1901, total global withdrawals sit below 500 billion cubic meters, with the OECD layer comprising the majority of this initial volume. Over the century, all three layers expand. The OECD band shows steady growth until roughly 1980, after which it stabilizes near the 1 trillion cubic meter mark through 2010. The BRICS layer begins as a thin band but widens significantly starting around 1950, continuing a steep upward trajectory to reach approximately 1.7 trillion cubic meters by the end of the period. The ROW layer, representing the rest of the world, also expands consistently, particularly from the mid-20th century onward. By 2010, the combined height of the three areas reaches just above 4 trillion cubic meters. The visual composition shifts over time; while OECD dominates the early years, the BRICS and ROW layers contribute increasingly larger shares to the total volume in the latter half of the timeline.
